data.title: Markets Price Higher Year-Ahead Inflation — Market Talk
data.description: Market-based estimates of inflation over the next 12 months have increased, data published by Barclays show. The rate on 1-year CPI swaps--a way for traders to bet on how they think the consumer-price index will change over roughly the next 12 months--has risen to around 3%, up from less than 2% six months ago. Since CPI came in at 3% in January, it's a sign that traders are skeptical inflation will make any further progress lower at all this year. That rhymes with the outlook from the Fed, whose officials penciled in 2.5% year-end PCE inflation for 2025 in December. Analysts are expecting PCE inflation to come in at about 2.5% for January--so the Fed estimates too suggest the possibility of no further inflation progress this year. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

data.title: AUDUSD falls as risk-off sentiment and technical sellers push the pair lower
data.description:
The AUDUSD moved higher yesterday as the USD weakened, bringing the pair within 5 pips of the 38.2% retracement level from the September 2024 high. Just above that, the falling 100-day moving average (MA) also acted as resistance. Sellers leaned into these levels, leading to a technical rotation lower.

As the session progressed, US equity markets came under increasing pressure, shifting the market's focus to risk-off sentiment. The NASDAQ is on pace for its course day since January 6, while the Dow 30 is on pace for its worst trading day since October 21.

The AUDUSD, which is sensitive to stock market declines and concerns over slower growth, became vulnerable to further downside pressure. Additionally, the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment report intensified fears about the economy, while rising inflation components added to the anxiety, accelerating the decline.

The pair has since retreated, trading back near early-week levels and last week's closing price. A week ago, the AUDUSD closed at 0.6350, and it is currently trading just above that at 0.6356, with today’s low reaching 0.6351.

Key Technical Levels:

  • Downside:
    • A key swing area near 0.6334 (previous support/resistance). A break below this level would open the door for a move toward the 100-bar MA on the 4-hour chart, between 0.6287 and 0.6301.
  • Upside:
    • A move above the 38.2% retracement level at 0.64139 would shift the bias to the bullish side, signaling potential further gains.

data.title: House of the Week: An Abandoned Barn Gets a Second Life in New York — WSJ
data.description:
By Libertina Brandt

The specs

  • List price: $3.25 million
  • Size: 5,000 square feet
  • 4 bedrooms, 5 bathrooms
  • Land: 13.36 acres

A barn becomes a home

Some buyers might view a dilapidated barn as a teardown. But when one hit the market in Germantown, N.Y., in 2009, Carolyn Roumeguere, a French jewelry designer, saw it as an opportunity.

"I walked in and put an offer in right there and then," she says, paying $385,000 for the property.

Once used as a summer getaway, Roumeguere discovered the barn had been abandoned for about 20 years by the time she bought it. It lacked insulation and the copper pipes had been stolen — but it had a bathroom, a bedroom and a small kitchen with a wood stove, she says.

Roumeguere, who grew up in Kenya and has sold jewelry to Barneys, Maxfield LA and Donna Karan, spent two years transforming the structure into her full-time home. She installed plumbing, electricity and heating, as well as three bedrooms and a loft area she uses as an art studio.

Roumeguere was told the barn was built in the 18th or 19th century due in part to its limestone foundation and slate roof. According to town historian Tom Shannon, the land where the home stands was once occupied by the family of John P. Rockefeller, a distant cousin of the Standard Oil Rockefellers. It is unclear if or when the family built the barn.

About three years after moving in, Roumeguere began building a roughly 1,800-square-foot, two-level guest barn on the property. It was completed in 2016.

Preserving the historic aesthetic

Working with local contractor Ricky Angier, Roumeguere restored the barn's original wood ceilings by removing plywood and sheetrock added in the 1950s. The flooring in what is now the main living room is also original.

The kitchen's wood flooring was rotting, so she swapped it with untreated pine, washing it with olive oil to seal it and give it a rustic, aged look. She also salvaged the barn's wood siding and used it as trim and as paneling for the interior walls. Roumeguere added insulation and replaced the roof's slate with metal.

On the top level of the guest barn, there's a bedroom suite with a bathroom and a private terrace. On the main level, there's a wraparound deck, a kitchen and a lounge area with a pool table that Roumeguere found at an antique shop.

The original renovation and the construction of the guest barn cost Roumeguere roughly $2 million. "I was creating my forever sanctuary," she said.

Can't-miss features

Roumeguere, who declined to share her age, built four fireplaces into the original barn, which took around 7,000 recycled bricks to complete. She visited antique shops in the Hudson Valley where she found Italian Carrara marble to make countertops. The wood for the kitchen island top came from an old factory, as did the light fixtures above it.

Outside, Roumeguere added a wraparound deck with a built-in sofa, an antique French copper bathtub and a custom table with a French wrought iron base and the same Carrara marble top used in the kitchen. The deck overlooks apple, sugar maple, oak and catalpa trees, which have heart-shaped leaves. "The whole property is very romantic," she says.

There are also pear, apricot and cherry trees, as well as vines that grow champagne grapes.

Reason for selling?

Roumeguere moved with her two children from Kenya to New York following political unrest in the late 2000s. She briefly listed her Germantown home in 2017 and planned to move to Texas but decided to stay. These days, she spends about 10 months a year in Kenya, which is why she decided to relist.

Market snapshot

This property is one of the most expensive for sale in Germantown, which is located in New York's Hudson Valley about 100 miles north of Manhattan. There are nine homes on the market with listing prices ranging from $299,000 to $3.25 million, according to Zillow. Late last year, the town saw two home sales over $3 million.

Annabel Taylor of Sotheby's International Realty has the listing.

Write to Libertina Brandt at Libertina.Brandt@wsj.com

data.title: Brent Settles Falls Over 2%
data.description:

data.title: Canada Core CPI Data Might Be 'Little Bit Overstated,' Bank of Canada Governor Says
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Bank of Canada Gov. Tiff Macklem said some technical features related to their preferred gauges of core inflation might be overstating the degree of price increases in the economy.

Consumer-price index data for January, released this week, indicated underlying, or core, inflation — which strips out volatile-priced items like food and energy — ticked higher, to 2.2%. The trimmed mean and weighted median measures of underlying inflation preferred by the Bank of Canada also heated up, averaging 2.7% annually compared with 2.55% in December.

This prompted some economists and traders to scale back rate-cut expectations for the rest of this year, after last month's quarter-point reduction in the Bank of Canada's policy rate.

At a press conference following a speech in suburban Toronto Friday, Macklem said broader gauges of underlying prices suggest core inflation is "a little over 2%. It doesn't look as high as our preferred measures of core would suggest."

He added later that some technical features related to the central bank's preferred core readings, such as how shelter costs are accounted for, suggest inflation "might be a little bit overstated" relative to official data.

During his speech, Macklem said that central bank officials are beginning work on reviewing the central bank's 2% inflation-targeting mandate, which comes up for renewal before the end of 2026. One of the elements Macklem said policymakers would closely look at is how core inflation is measured, and whether the central bank is using the appropriate gauges.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: Corn Futures Fall on Sentiment Around Planting - Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Corn for May delivery fell 1.6%, to $5.04 3/4 a bushel, on the Chicago Board of Trade on Friday, as analysts and traders speculate that U.S. farmers will plant more corn than initially anticipated.
  • Soybeans for May delivery fell 0.5%, to $10.57 1/4 a bushel.
  • Wheat for May delivery rose 0.7%, to $6.04 1/2 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Getting More in the Ground: Corn futures turned lower amid speculation that U.S. farmers will plant more corn than previously expected. "Corn is clearly showing some signs of weakness setting in on several fronts," said Sterling Smith of StoneX. "First ideas about seeing bigger than expected corn acres are keeping the buyers to the sidelines...the solid recent run up is moving grain off the farm which is adding to the pressure."

Slimming Crop: Wheat futures rose Friday, with traders looking to the Black Sea for indications on how prices should move. "The Russia quota is signaling a concern that traders have had about the quality of both the Russian and Ukrainian crop," said Phil Flynn of Price Futures Group. Flynn adds that other crops globally have struggled, tightening the overall supply and demand picture. "The global wheat market crop has been poor--not only in Europe, but...about the U.S. deep freeze and its impact on winter wheat."

INSIGHT

Watering the Plants: Corn and soybean futures on the CBOT were down Friday, as the weather outlook for the U.S. Corn Belt looks supportive for crops being planted this spring. Precipitation over the next two weeks is expected to be above average, said the Climate Prediction Center in its latest data. Analysts and traders believe the moisture will be beneficial for farmers when they plant crops. With traders reconsidering their outlook for the number of acres planted this spring, the weather forecast is a pressure point.

Lagging Behind: Energy futures in general were the chief focus for commodities traders this week, with natural gas and oil futures trending higher while others like grains lagged behind. According to data compiled by Saxo Bank, energy futures gained over 4% this week, while metals and grains gained under 2% and softs lost roughly 1%. But overall, gains in the Bloomberg Commodity Total Return Index are outpacing the S&P 500 amid President Trump's tariff push, said Ole Hansen of Saxo Bank in a note. "The near future points to heightened uncertainty," he said. "So far, commodities have emerged as one of the winners amid increased demand for key raw materials, which could face supply challenges."

AHEAD

  • The USDA will release its weekly Grain Export Inspections report at 11 a.m. ET Monday.
  • The EIA will release its weekly ethanol production and stocks report at 10:30 a.m. ET Wednesday.
  • The USDA will release its monthly Cold Storage report at 3 p.m. ET Wednesday.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: JPMorgan Chase Branch Expansion Portends Deposit Growth — Market Talk
data.description: JPMorgan Chase's brick-and-mortar expansion promises to drive more dominance for the bank, Truist analysts say. JPM has by far the most branches younger than 10 years old (around 1,100 vs. roughly 500 at Bank of America, which is closest behind), as it has spread to the entire continental U.S. over the past 7 years. It generally takes more than 5 years for bank deposits at new branches to meet the level of mature ones, so JPM has a long runway of opportunity, which analysts estimate could be around 15% in retail deposit growth. The bank is still expanding, with 500 new builds planned by 2027. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1515 ET - JPMorgan Chase's brick-and-mortar expansion promises to drive more dominance for the bank, Truist analysts say. JPM has by far the most branches younger than 10 years old (around 1,100 vs. roughly 500 at Bank of America, which is closest behind), as it has spread to the entire continental U.S. over the past 7 years. It generally takes more than 5 years for bank deposits at new branches to meet the level of mature ones, so JPM has a long runway of opportunity, which analysts estimate could be around 15% in retail deposit growth. The bank is still expanding, with 500 new builds planned by 2027. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1458 ET - U.S. natural gas futures post a 14% weekly gain as an Arctic blast lifted heating demand across much of the country, while deliveries to LNG export terminals hit new highs above 16 billion cubic feet a day. Cheniere Energy's announcement of a first cargo from its Corpus Christi Stage 3 expansion was "big news for LNG," Gelber &Associates says in a note. While milder temperatures next week are expected to lower natural gas demand, and production is seen picking up after freeze-offs, prices are expected to find support in the growing storage deficit given this winter's hefty withdrawals. The Nymex front month settles up 2% at $4.234/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1355 ET - Booking Holdings blew past expectations in 4Q, fueled by global healthy travel demand and the continued execution of its strategic initiatives, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. The analysts expect the parent of Booking.com and Kayak to continue the run in 2025, in part citing the company's growing direct and loyalty bookings, as well as its lean into generative AI. These efforts should drive multi-year double-digit percent EPS growth, they write. Booking says demand is continuing to hold up so far in 2025, and that it will lean more heavily into social media marketing, particularly in partnership with Meta Platforms. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1257 ET - Celsius' $1.8 billion buy of Alani Nu should complement the company, William Blair analysts say in a research note, citing the rapidly growing energy drink brand's appeal to female consumers, as well as its relevance with Gen Z and Millennial consumers. The deal, which is expected to be accretive to per-share earnings within a year, will also alleviate competition risks, they add. The acquisition comes as Celsius says it is confident in its prospects for 2025, despite not providing earnings guidance. The company is launching new flavors, expanding into new categories and seeking to increase its distribution, which the analysts say should add to its already attractive position. Celsius surges 26% following a 4Q earnings beat. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1249 ET - AppLovin remains the clear leader in mobile game advertising, even after Unity debuted an AI-driven ad platform this week, Benchmark analyst Mike Hickey says. Unity on Thursday announced the launch of Vector, a self-learning ad platform aimed at improving ad performance on mobile games. AppLovin's stock is off 14% since Wednesday's close as investors worry about tightening competition, but Hickey says it is still much further along in development than Unity. Vector is set to roll out in 1Q and is effectively a start-over in ad monetization, Hickey says, while AppLovin is already monetizing and scaling its platform. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1238 ET - While the demand recovery timeline in furniture and furnishings has taken a lot longer than anticipated, the risk/reward set-up on Wayfair remains very compelling, Raymond James analysts say in a research note. The online furniture retailer continues to outperform its peers and has created a leaner P&L to drive meaningful Ebitda growth as demand returns. "We believe Wayfair has the right playbook," the analysts say, adding that the furniture industry is positioned better to modestly improve throughout 2025 after multiple years of pressure. Raymond James raises its 1Q adjusted Ebitda estimate to $81 million from $73 million, but lowers its FY25 adjusted Ebitda views to $474 million from $481 million previously on a 0.8% increase in annual revenue growth. Shares fall 6.7% to $43.(sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1224 ET - Bluebird bio's deal to be taken private will leave investors with a rich reward or a steep haircut, depending on sales of the company's gene therapies over the next three years. Analysts at William Blair think it will be the latter. Bluebird holders will get an initial $3 a share, 57% below Thursday's closing price, but contingent value rights that would be triggered by 12-month sales of $600 million would bring another $6.84 a share, for a 40% premium. William Blair says it currently models bluebird's sales reaching only $546.4 million in 2027, making the probability of achieving the CVR contingency low. Bluebird slide 39% to $4.29. (colin.kellaher@wsj.com)

1147 ET - Rivian analysts don't seem as concerned as the rest of the market about the conservative EV deliveries guidance that the carmaker issued Thursday after its 4Q report. The company's 4Q results easily topped expectations and show Rivian achieving its goal of a positive gross profit as vehicle costs come down, Stifel analysts say in a research note. That offsets some negatives, including seasonality, a challenging demand environment and the L.A. fires, that have it guiding for fewer deliveries in 1Q than expected, the analysts say. "Overall, we view the print and outlook as about neutral for the shares near-term," they say, maintaining a buy rating. Analysts from Wedbush and Benchmark are also maintaining bullish ratings on the stock. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1140 ET - Rivian is on track for long-term growth, but it faces political uncertainty over the regulatory environment for EVs, Benchmark analyst Mickey Legg says in a research note. The Republican administration in power could repeal EV tax credits and other incentives, which would delay growth in the EV sector, the analyst says. Donald Trump has signed an executive order instructing agencies to stop distributing funds allocated for EV development and cancelling his predecessor's goal for 50% of new vehicle sales by 2030 to be EVs. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1129 ET - Dye & Durham's new management names its second interim CEO since the board overthrow in December. After the activist investor Engine Capital successfully had its candidates appointed to the board at the end of last year, the Canadian cloud-based platform named Hans Gieskes the new chairman and interim chief executive. On Friday, Gieskes' roles were removed and distributed to two other candidates, Sid Singh taking over as the new interim chief executive, effective immediately and Engine founder and portfolio manager Arnaud Ajdler as chair. Gieskes will remain on the board. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1118 ET - News that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration declared the shortage of Novo Nordisk's weight-loss and diabetes drugs Wegovy and Ozempic over marks an important step toward a normalized obesity-drug market in the U.S., Barclays analysts say in a note. The move should eventually lead to pharmacies or physicians no longer producing copies of the branded drugs, Barclays says. Nevertheless, the exact date when copycat versions of the drugs come off market remains somewhat uncertain, the analysts say. The FDA designation shows the Danish pharmaceutical company's efforts to scale up production have reached a level that it can now supply the market for type 2 diabetes and obesity, Barclays says. Shares rise 5.8%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1054 ET - Sika expects a difficult year for its automotive business in Europe, CEO Thomas Hasler says in an interview. The Switzerland-based specialty-chemical company is running at the output level for cars in Europe, which is far below the usual going rate, indicating a huge backlog, Hasler says. "Consumers are hesitant to invest. The electrification path is somehow stalled and incentives are not in place to stimulate," he says. However, the fleet in Europe is aging drastically and will have to recover in the years to come, Hasler says. This year is probably still going to see declining volumes, also given the geopolitical uncertainty, he adds. Shares trade 0.5% higher at 236.70 Swiss francs. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

By Randall W. Forsyth

Nobody goes there anymore, it's too crowded. So said that late, great philosopher, Yogi Berra.

Case in point: Europe. And it isn't just Barcelona, where locals protested the hordes of tourists last summer by spraying them with water guns.

Some contrarian-minded investors have rushed into European stock markets in recent months, notwithstanding the persistently sluggish economies on the continent, resulting in strong gains in those bourses. Most likely, they were drawn to Europe by the equities' extreme undervaluation after longtime laggard returns. On that score, the MSCI EMU index of large- and mid-cap European stocks trades for about 14 times expected earnings for the next 12 months, less than two-thirds the 22 times forward price/earnings multiple on the S&P 500 index.

Many of these investment "tourists" now find they have too much company and are taking their winnings and going home. But more experienced global investors can still find compelling values among individual European stocks rather than the broad exchange-traded funds that first attracted the tourists.

For all the talk of American exceptionalism, European stocks have outpaced U.S. stock indexes of late, and that's despite the dominance of the big technology names among the latter. Among major ETFs of the sort that would populate Barron's readers' portfolios, the Vanguard FTSE Europe ETF has returned 9.96% in 2025 through Wednesday, according to Morningstar data, better than twice the 4.61% year-to-date return of the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO). The iShares MSCI Germany ETF (EWG) has returned an even greater 13.32% so far this year.

Bank of America's latest survey of fund managers, released this past week, found that 12% of respondents were overweight European stocks, the highest percentage of accounts since last June. While relatively low, that still represented a big swing from 36% having been underweight Europe just two months earlier, as bargain hunters were attracted to the vastly cheaper valuations of European equities compared with the Magnificent Seven stocks that dominate the U.S. market.

That investors would venture to Europe might seem counterintuitive, especially since Germany, the continent's largest economy, is facing its third year of recession and has a crucial election ahead this Sunday. Still, the DAX hit a record this past Tuesday, and was up 28.5% from a year ago at Thursday's close.

Neither have European stocks suffered from the America-first policies of President Donald Trump. Indeed, in a perhaps perverse way, Trump is pushing Europe in the right direction, said David Herro, lead manager of the $4.2 billion Oakmark International fund (OAKIX). Herro is a veteran value investor who has long favored European stocks.

For one thing, Trump has insisted that Europe will have to pay more for its own defense, which has made defense-equipment stocks the leaders of the rally, said Herro. Plus, the prospects for an end to the Russia-Ukraine conflict could lower energy costs sharply, especially the price of natural gas (although that seems a meager payoff for Trump's Orwellian assertion that Ukraine started the war, which The Wall Street Journal's editorial page said " mimicked Russian propaganda").

There are signs that American investors may be all in on Europe. Part of the influx was an "unwind of the U.S. exceptionalism theme," according to a research report by the J.P. Morgan global markets strategy team led by Nikolaos Panigirtzoglou. That has led to a markedly overbought technical condition in the Eurostoxx 50 index of big, blue-chip sector leaders, which can persist, especially if retail investors continue to pile in, the team wrote.

So far this year, investment flows tracked by the bank have been heading into Europe. European equity flow has returned to its high of 2023 from close to a maximum short position in late 2024, and is near the level of early February 2022, before Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

Some European stocks may be a bit too rich now. Year-to-date gains "look extended" among potential beneficiaries of a Russia-Ukraine cease-fire, the JPM note said, such as companies that would benefit from reconstruction and those that would be helped by lower European Union gas prices. That's in line with the observation from a bank trading desk that the momentum in European stocks may be waning.

But Herro still sees compelling values, noting that "companies aren't countries." In other words, it matters more where a firm does business than where it is domiciled.

Mercedes-Benz Group, whose shares slumped 2.5% Thursday (after having risen nearly 20% since mid-November) is one example. While the auto maker warned of lower profit margins owing to sluggish global demand, especially in China, where it faces tough competition, Herro cited Mercedes' financial strength — specifically 31 billion euros ($32.4 billion) of cash on its balance sheet, relative to a stock-market capitalization of EUR57 billion.

Mercedes pays a healthy dividend yield of 6%. Plus, it will be using its cash to buy back 10% of the shares, Herro said.

Financial stocks also have been among the leaders in the European rally, and Herro favors BNP Paribas, which he calls one of highest-quality institution that "ranks right up there" with JPMorgan Chase. Paribas' shares trade at about seven times earnings and at 70% of book value, while the big French bank has been posting a strong return on equity of 13% to 14%. Paribas pays a dividend yield of 6.8%.

By comparison, JPMorgan Chase trades for 2.3 times book and 13.3 times earnings, and yields less than one-third as much, or 1.9%, Herro noted.

Value has always been the attraction of big European stocks, although it has been diminished a bit by the recent rally. Yet while some of the short-term trading gains may be behind European shares, and uncertainties regarding U.S. tariffs loom, this trade isn't too crowded for select names like those Herro favors.

Write to Randall W. Forsyth at randall.forsyth@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Oil Rig Count Grows by 7; Crude Futures Headed for Weekly Losses
data.description:
The number of oil rigs in the US rose by seven in the week ended Friday, according to data compiled by energy services company Baker Hughes .

The count for oil increased to 488 rigs from 481 last week, while gas lost two rigs to 99. The number of miscellaneous rigs fell by one to five. The US had 503 oil, 120 gas and three miscellaneous rigs in operation a year earlier, the data showed.

Overall, 592 rigs were operating in the US as of Friday, down from 626 a year earlier. Among US states, Oklahoma added five rigs sequentially, while New Mexico and Utah lost one each.

Across North America, oil and gas rigs rose by three to 836 from a week earlier. The tally in Canada dropped by one to 244.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil was down 3% at $70.31 a barrel in late trading Friday, while Brent fell 2.8% to $74.32. Both were headed for weekly losses.

The market is dealing with "an increasingly erratic message flow from Washington, which on one hand has raised the prospect of increased US production, while on the other causing concerns about the outlook for global growth and demand," Saxo Bank Head of Commodity Strategy Ole Hansen said in a report published Friday.

Markets are also awaiting a decision from the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries and its allies on output, according to Hansen.

"With limited clarity on the impact of current US threats and policies, as well as the outlook for key producers such as Russia and Iran, we believe the group is likely to tread with caution, leaving production unchanged while once again reiterating the need for compliance," Hansen said.

On Thursday, data from the Energy Information Administration showed that commercial crude stockpiles in the US jumped more than projected last week.

data.title: Boston Beer Likely to Deliver Soft Q4 Volumes Amid Category Pressure, RBC Says
data.description:
Boston Beer is likely to deliver another quarter of "soft volume performance" in Q4, largely due to broader category pressure in the beer business, RBC Capital Markets said in an earnings preview Friday.

According to the firm, category softness is weighing on the company's Twisted Tea brand, which continues to grow but at a slowing pace.

Meanwhile, the Truly brand is still losing share in the hard seltzer category and "materially underperforming" White Claw, though its sequential volume trends have improved. RBC also noted that while Sun Cruiser is "doing well," it is not yet significant enough to drive "meaningful upside at this point."

The firm views the risk-to-reward heading into the company's Q4 earnings as negative, citing sluggish topline trends that will likely pressure numbers through the first half of 2025.

"[Boston Beer] is still small enough to gain share in a difficult category but at this point we are not expecting anything material," according to the note.

For 2025, the firm projects "modest" volume growth in a range of negative low single digits to positive low single digits, with 1-2% pricing growth on the topline.

RBC has a sector perform rating on the stock with a price target of $295.
